cards.definition: the number of protons in an atom — defines the element. Also called proton number
how electrons are arranged in shells — written as 2.8.1 for sodium
vertical columns — elements in the same group have the same number of outer electrons
horizontal rows — each period represents one more electron shell
Li, Na, K — very reactive metals — react vigorously with water producing hydrogen gas
F, Cl, Br, I — reactive non-metals — reactivity decreases down the group
He, Ne, Ar — full outer shells — very unreactive, colourless gases
block of metals in the middle of the periodic table — high melting points, coloured compounds, catalysts
weighted average mass of an atom compared to 1/12 the mass of carbon-12
shows only the ions that change during a reaction — with state symbols

questions.question: What is atomic number?
options: A) Number of neutrons
B) Mass of atom
C) Number of protons — defines the element
D) Number of electrons only
questions.explanation: Atomic number = number of protons = number of electrons in a neutral atom.

questions.question: What is electronic configuration?
options: A) Charge of atom
B) How electrons are arranged in shells — e.g. 2.8.1 for sodium
C) Number of neutrons
D) Mass of an electron
questions.explanation: Electrons fill shells from inside out — first shell holds 2, second holds 8, third holds 8.

questions.question: What do elements in the same group have in common?
options: A) Same mass number
B) Same number of neutrons
C) Same number of outer electrons — similar chemical properties
D) Same period
questions.explanation: Group number = number of outer electrons. Same group = same outer electrons = similar properties.

questions.question: What are the properties of alkali metals?
options: A) Unreactive dense metals
B) Very reactive — react with water producing hydrogen
C) Non-metals with high melting points
D) Coloured gases
questions.explanation: Group 1 metals (Li, Na, K) react with water: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂. Reactivity increases down the group.

questions.question: What characterises noble gases?
options: A) Very reactive non-metals
B) Metals with high melting points
C) Full outer electron shells — very unreactive
D) Alkali metals
questions.explanation: Noble gases have full outer shells — no tendency to gain or lose electrons.

questions.question: What is the reactivity trend of halogens?
options: A) Increases down Group 7
B) Decreases down Group 7
C) Same throughout the group
D) No trend
questions.explanation: Halogen reactivity decreases down Group 7 (F > Cl > Br > I) — outer shell gets further from nucleus, less easily gained.

questions.question: What are transition metals known for?
options: A) Low melting points
B) Being unreactive gases
C) High melting points, coloured compounds, acting as catalysts
D) Reacting vigorously with water
questions.explanation: Transition metals (Fe, Cu, Ni) have high melting points, form multiple ions, have coloured compounds, and act as catalysts.

questions.question: What does period number indicate?
options: A) Number of electrons
B) Number of electron shells
C) Number of protons only
D) No relationship to shells
questions.explanation: Period 1 = 1 shell, Period 2 = 2 shells, Period 3 = 3 shells.

questions.question: What is relative atomic mass?
options: A) Mass of protons only
B) Weighted average mass compared to 1/12 mass of carbon-12
C) Sum of protons and electrons
D) Number of neutrons
questions.explanation: Ar accounts for isotopes — calculated using masses and % abundances of all isotopes.

questions.question: What does an ionic equation show?
options: A) All atoms including spectator ions
B) Only the ions that change — with state symbols
C) Molecular formula of reactants
D) Energy change of the reaction
questions.explanation: Ionic equations omit spectator ions: e.g. H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l).

questions.question: Atomic number equals the number of protons.
questions.explanation: Atomic number = protons = electrons in a neutral atom.

questions.question: Elements in the same group have different numbers of outer electrons.
questions.explanation: Same GROUP = same outer electrons = similar properties.

questions.question: Noble gases are very reactive.
questions.explanation: Noble gases are very UNREACTIVE — full outer shells.

questions.question: Reactivity of halogens increases down Group 7.
questions.explanation: Halogen reactivity DECREASES down Group 7.

questions.question: Alkali metals react with water producing hydrogen.
questions.explanation: 2Na + 2H₂O → 2NaOH + H₂.

questions.question: Period number equals number of electron shells.
questions.explanation: Period 3 elements have 3 electron shells.

questions.question: Transition metals have low melting points.
questions.explanation: Transition metals have HIGH melting points.

questions.question: Relative atomic mass accounts for isotopes.
questions.explanation: Ar is the weighted average of all naturally occurring isotopes.

questions.question: Ionic equations include spectator ions.
questions.explanation: Ionic equations OMIT spectator ions.

questions.question: Electronic configuration shows electron arrangement in shells.
questions.explanation: E.g. sodium: 2.8.1.

questions.question: ________ number = number of protons — defines the element.
questions.explanation: Also called proton number in Edexcel.

questions.question: Electronic ________ shows electron arrangement — e.g. 2.8.1 for sodium.
questions.explanation: Outer electrons determine chemical properties.

questions.question: Elements in the same ________ have the same number of outer electrons.
questions.explanation: Group number = number of outer electrons.

questions.question: ________ metals (Group 1) react vigorously with water — reactivity increases down group.
questions.explanation: Li < Na < K in reactivity.

questions.question: ________ (Group 7) are reactive non-metals — reactivity decreases down the group.
questions.explanation: F₂ > Cl₂ > Br₂ > I₂.

questions.question: Noble gases (Group 0) are ________ — full outer electron shells.
questions.explanation: No tendency to gain or lose electrons.

questions.question: ________ metals form coloured compounds and act as catalysts.
questions.explanation: Iron (Haber process), platinum (catalytic converters).

questions.question: ________ number = number of electron shells.
questions.explanation: Period 3 elements have 3 electron shells.

questions.question: Relative atomic ________ accounts for all isotopes and their abundances.
questions.explanation: Ar = Σ(isotope mass × % abundance) ÷ 100.

questions.question: ________ equations show only the ions that change — spectator ions omitted.
questions.explanation: E.g. Ag⁺(aq) + Cl⁻(aq) → AgCl(s).

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: number of protons — defines the element
Group 1 — react with water making hydrogen

cards.definition: transfer of electrons from metal to non-metal — forms positive and negative ions attracted to each other
regular 3D arrangement of oppositely charged ions — high melting point, conducts when molten/dissolved
sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms — e.g. H₂, H₂O, CH₄
covalent molecules with weak intermolecular forces — low melting/boiling points, do not conduct electricity
network of covalent bonds — very high melting point — e.g. diamond, silicon dioxide
positive metal ions in a sea of delocalised electrons — good electrical and thermal conductor
giant covalent carbon — each carbon bonded to 4 others — very hard, high melting point, does not conduct
layered giant covalent carbon — each carbon bonded to 3 others — layers can slide, conducts electricity
particles 1–100 nm in size — very high surface area:volume ratio — used in medicine, sunscreens, electronics
a long chain molecule made of repeating monomer units — e.g. polythene, nylon

questions.question: What forms in ionic bonding?
options: A) Shared electron pairs
B) Positive and negative ions attracted to each other — after electron transfer
C) Metallic bonds
D) Covalent bonds
questions.explanation: Ionic bonding: metal loses electrons (positive ion) + non-metal gains electrons (negative ion) → electrostatic attraction.

questions.question: What is covalent bonding?
options: A) Transfer of electrons from metal to non-metal
B) Sharing of electron pairs between non-metal atoms
C) Attraction between opposite ions
D) Sea of delocalised electrons
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds form between non-metals — atoms share electron pairs to achieve full outer shells.

questions.question: Why do ionic compounds have high melting points?
options: A) Strong covalent bonds
B) Strong electrostatic attraction between many oppositely charged ions in the lattice
C) Metallic bonding
D) Weak intermolecular forces
questions.explanation: Ionic lattices have strong electrostatic forces in all directions — a lot of energy is needed to break them apart.

questions.question: Why does graphite conduct electricity but diamond does not?
options: A) Diamond has free electrons; graphite does not
B) Graphite has delocalised electrons that carry charge; diamond has no free electrons
C) Both conduct equally
D) Diamond is an ionic compound
questions.explanation: In graphite, each carbon forms 3 bonds — leaving one delocalised electron per atom free to carry charge. In diamond, all 4 electrons are in bonds.

questions.question: What are nanoparticles?
options: A) Particles too large to be useful
B) Particles 1–100 nm — very high SA:V ratio — used in medicine and electronics
C) Only naturally occurring particles
D) Particles made only of carbon
questions.explanation: Nanoparticles have a huge surface area relative to their volume — this gives them unique properties compared to bulk materials of the same substance.

questions.question: Why do simple molecular compounds have low melting points?
options: A) Strong covalent bonds are broken
B) Weak intermolecular forces between molecules — easily overcome
C) Ionic bonds break easily
D) No bonds to break
questions.explanation: Simple molecular compounds: low melting/boiling points because only weak intermolecular forces (not covalent bonds) need to be broken.

questions.question: What is metallic bonding?
options: A) Transfer of electrons between metals
B) Positive metal ions in a sea of delocalised electrons
C) Covalent bonds between metal atoms
D) Ionic attraction between metal ions
questions.explanation: Metallic bonding: delocalised electrons move freely — explain electrical/thermal conductivity and malleability of metals.

questions.question: What is a polymer?
options: A) A single covalent molecule
B) A long chain molecule made of repeating monomer units
C) An ionic compound
D) A type of nanoparticle
questions.explanation: Polymers: long chain molecules made from many small monomers joined together — e.g. polythene from ethene monomers.

questions.question: Why is diamond very hard?
options: A) Weak bonds between layers
B) Ionic bonds throughout
C) Each carbon atom is bonded to 4 others — rigid 3D covalent network
D) Delocalised electrons
questions.explanation: Diamond has a giant covalent structure with each carbon bonded to 4 others — every bond must be broken to deform it.

questions.question: What is the difference between diamond and graphite?
options: A) Diamond conducts; graphite does not
B) Both have identical structures
C) Diamond: 4 bonds per carbon, no conductivity; graphite: 3 bonds per carbon, conducts
D) Graphite has a 3D network; diamond has layers
questions.explanation: Diamond: 4 covalent bonds, tetrahedral, very hard, no free electrons. Graphite: 3 covalent bonds, layered, soft, has free electrons.

questions.question: Ionic bonding involves sharing electrons.
questions.explanation: Ionic bonding involves TRANSFER of electrons — covalent bonding involves sharing.

questions.question: Ionic compounds conduct electricity when solid.
questions.explanation: Ionic compounds conduct only when MOLTEN or DISSOLVED — ions must be free to move.

questions.question: Simple molecular compounds have high melting points.
questions.explanation: Simple molecular compounds have LOW melting points — weak intermolecular forces are easily broken.

questions.question: Graphite conducts electricity because it has delocalised electrons.
questions.explanation: Each carbon in graphite forms 3 bonds — leaving one delocalised electron per atom.

questions.question: Nanoparticles have a low surface area to volume ratio.
questions.explanation: Nanoparticles have a VERY HIGH SA:V ratio — giving them unique properties.

questions.question: Metallic bonding explains why metals conduct electricity.
questions.explanation: Delocalised electrons in metallic bonds carry electrical charge.

questions.question: Diamond is soft because its bonds between layers are weak.
questions.explanation: Diamond has NO layers — it is hard because of its rigid 3D covalent network. Graphite has weak forces between layers.

questions.question: Polymers are made of repeating monomer units.
questions.explanation: Polymerisation joins many small monomer molecules into long polymer chains.

questions.question: Covalent bonds involve transfer of electrons.
questions.explanation: Covalent bonds involve SHARING electrons — transfer is ionic bonding.

questions.question: Giant covalent structures have very high melting points.
questions.explanation: Giant covalent structures (diamond, SiO₂) have very high melting points — many strong covalent bonds must be broken.

questions.question: ________ bonding involves transfer of electrons — positive and negative ions attract.
questions.explanation: Metal loses electrons (+ion); non-metal gains electrons (−ion).

questions.question: ________ bonding involves sharing electron pairs between non-metals.
questions.explanation: Shared electrons give both atoms full outer shells.

questions.question: An ionic ________ is a regular 3D arrangement of oppositely charged ions.
questions.explanation: Strong electrostatic forces in all directions = high melting point.

questions.question: Simple ________ structures have low melting points — weak intermolecular forces.
questions.explanation: Weak forces between molecules are easily overcome.

questions.question: ________ structures have very high melting points — e.g. diamond, silicon dioxide.
questions.explanation: All atoms bonded by strong covalent bonds throughout.

questions.question: ________ bonding: positive ions in a sea of delocalised electrons — conducts electricity.
questions.explanation: Delocalised electrons carry charge and heat.

questions.question: ________ has 4 bonds per carbon — rigid 3D network — very hard, no conductivity.
questions.explanation: All 4 outer electrons form bonds — no free electrons to carry charge.

questions.question: ________ has 3 bonds per carbon — layered — soft, conducts electricity.
questions.explanation: One delocalised electron per carbon — layers slide over each other.

questions.question: ________ are 1–100 nm particles with very high SA:V ratio.
questions.explanation: Used in medicine, sunscreens, and electronics.

questions.question: A ________ is a long chain molecule made of repeating monomer units.
questions.explanation: E.g. polythene from ethene, nylon from diamine and diacid.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: electron transfer — positive + negative ions — high mp
4 bonds per C — rigid — hard — no conductivity
3 bonds per C — layers — conducts electricity
delocalised electrons — conducts electricity and heat

cards.definition: a substance that produces H⁺ ions in solution — pH below 7
a soluble base — produces OH⁻ ions in solution — pH above 7
acid + base → salt + water. H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O
an ionic compound formed when an acid reacts with a base — e.g. sodium chloride, copper sulfate
order of metals by reactivity: K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Cu > Ag > Au
a more reactive metal displaces a less reactive metal from its compound
using electrical energy to decompose an ionic compound — ions move to electrodes
loss of electrons (or gain of oxygen). OIL — Oxidation Is Loss
gain of electrons (or loss of oxygen). RIG — Reduction Is Gain
unreactive metals found native; metals above carbon extracted by electrolysis; others reduced by carbon

questions.question: What is neutralisation?
options: A) Dissolving a metal in acid
B) Acid + base → salt + water. H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O
C) Electrolysis of a salt solution
D) A displacement reaction
questions.explanation: Neutralisation: the H⁺ from the acid reacts with the OH⁻ from the base — producing water and a salt.

questions.question: What is the reactivity series?
options: A) Order of metals by mass
B) Order of metals by reactivity — K most reactive, Au least
C) Order of metals by melting point
D) Order of metals by electrical conductivity
questions.explanation: The reactivity series: K, Na, Ca, Mg, Al, C (non-metal), Zn, Fe, H (non-metal), Cu, Ag, Au.

questions.question: What is a displacement reaction?
options: A) Acid reacting with a metal
B) A more reactive metal displacing a less reactive metal from its compound
C) Electrolysis of molten salt
D) Combustion of a metal
questions.explanation: E.g. Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu. Zinc is more reactive than copper — it displaces copper from its compound.

questions.question: What happens at the cathode in electrolysis?
options: A) Oxidation — positive ions are repelled
B) Reduction — positive ions (cations) gain electrons
C) Negative ions are discharged
D) Oxygen is produced
questions.explanation: At the cathode (negative electrode): cations (positive ions) gain electrons — reduction occurs.

questions.question: What does OIL RIG stand for?
options: A) Oxidation Is Loss; Reduction Is Gain of electrons
B) Oxidation Increases Levels; Reduction Increases Growth
C) Only In Labs; Rarely In General
D) Oxygen Is Lost; Reduction Is Gained
questions.explanation: OIL RIG: Oxidation Is Loss (of electrons); Reduction Is Gain (of electrons). Also: oxidation = gain of oxygen; reduction = loss of oxygen.

questions.question: Why are reactive metals extracted by electrolysis?
options: A) Carbon is cheaper than electrolysis
B) Reactive metals cannot be reduced by carbon — they are more reactive than carbon
C) Electrolysis is always the cheapest method
D) Reactive metals are found native
questions.explanation: Metals above carbon in the reactivity series (K, Na, Ca, Al) cannot be reduced by carbon — electrolysis is needed.

questions.question: What is an acid?
options: A) A substance with pH above 7
B) A substance that produces H⁺ ions in solution — pH below 7
C) A substance that produces OH⁻ ions
D) Any substance that dissolves in water
questions.explanation: Acids produce hydrogen ions (H⁺) in aqueous solution. Strong acids fully ionise (HCl); weak acids partially ionise (CH₃COOH).

questions.question: What is formed when an acid reacts with a metal carbonate?
options: A) Salt + water
B) Salt + water + carbon dioxide
C) Salt + hydrogen
D) Just carbon dioxide
questions.explanation: Acid + metal carbonate → salt + water + CO₂. E.g. HCl + CaCO₃ → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂.

questions.question: What is oxidation in terms of electrons?
options: A) Gain of electrons
B) Loss of electrons
C) Transfer of protons
D) Gain of oxygen only
questions.explanation: Oxidation = Loss of electrons (OIL). Reduction = Gain of electrons (RIG).

questions.question: Which metal can be found native (uncombined) in nature?
options: A) Aluminium
B) Iron
C) Gold
D) Sodium
questions.explanation: Gold is very unreactive — it does not combine with other elements and is found as the pure metal. Most metals are found as ores (compounds).

questions.question: Neutralisation produces salt and water.
questions.explanation: Acid + base → salt + water. H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O.

questions.question: A more reactive metal displaces a less reactive one in a displacement reaction.
questions.explanation: E.g. zinc displaces copper from copper sulfate solution.

questions.question: Reduction involves loss of electrons.
questions.explanation: Reduction involves GAIN of electrons. Oxidation involves loss (OIL RIG).

questions.question: Reactive metals like sodium are extracted by reduction with carbon.
questions.explanation: Metals MORE reactive than carbon (Na, K, Al) are extracted by ELECTROLYSIS.

questions.question: Acids produce H⁺ ions in solution.
questions.explanation: Strong acids fully ionise; weak acids partially ionise.

questions.question: At the cathode in electrolysis, oxidation occurs.
questions.explanation: At the CATHODE: reduction (positive ions gain electrons). At the ANODE: oxidation.

questions.question: The reactivity series puts potassium as the most reactive common metal.
questions.explanation: K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Cu > Ag > Au.

questions.question: Acid + carbonate produces salt + water + hydrogen.
questions.explanation: Acid + carbonate produces salt + water + CARBON DIOXIDE.

questions.question: Gold is found native (uncombined) in the Earth.
questions.explanation: Gold's very low reactivity means it doesn't combine with other elements.

questions.question: Electrolysis uses electrical energy to decompose ionic compounds.
questions.explanation: Electrolysis breaks down ionic compounds using electrical current.

questions.question: ________ is acid + base → salt + water.
questions.explanation: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O.

questions.question: A ________ reaction: more reactive metal displaces less reactive metal from its compound.
questions.explanation: E.g. Zn + CuSO₄ → ZnSO₄ + Cu.

questions.question: ________ uses electrical energy to decompose ionic compounds.
questions.explanation: At cathode: reduction. At anode: oxidation.

questions.question: OIL RIG: Oxidation Is ________, Reduction Is Gain of electrons.
questions.explanation: Essential memory aid for redox reactions.

questions.question: Metals more reactive than carbon are extracted by ________.
questions.explanation: Na, K, Ca, and Al require electrolysis.

questions.question: The ________ series orders metals from most to least reactive.
questions.explanation: K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Cu > Ag > Au.

questions.question: ________ + carbonate → salt + water + carbon dioxide.
questions.explanation: CO₂ gas is produced — can be tested with limewater.

questions.question: At the ________ electrode in electrolysis, positive ions gain electrons.
questions.explanation: Cathode = negative electrode = reduction.

questions.question: An ________ is a substance that produces H⁺ ions in solution — pH below 7.
questions.explanation: Strong acids fully ionise; weak acids partially ionise.

questions.question: ________ is the gain of oxygen or loss of electrons.
questions.explanation: OIL: Oxidation Is Loss.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: K > Na > Ca > Mg > Al > Zn > Fe > Cu > Ag > Au
Oxidation Is Loss; Reduction Is Gain of electrons
more reactive metal replaces less reactive metal

cards.definition: how fast a chemical reaction occurs — measured by change in mass, volume of gas, or colour
particles must collide with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy) for a reaction to occur
the minimum energy needed for a successful collision — lowered by a catalyst
a substance that speeds up a reaction by lowering activation energy — not consumed
releases heat energy — temperature of surroundings increases — e.g. combustion, neutralisation
absorbs heat energy — temperature of surroundings decreases — e.g. thermal decomposition
a mixture of hydrocarbons formed from ancient marine organisms — a finite fossil fuel
separation of crude oil into fractions by heating — fractions condense at different temperatures
saturated hydrocarbons: CₙH₂ₙ₊₂ — only single C-C bonds — e.g. methane (CH₄), ethane (C₂H₆)
breaking down long-chain hydrocarbons into shorter, more useful molecules — produces alkenes

questions.question: What does collision theory state?
options: A) All collisions lead to reactions
B) Particles must collide with sufficient energy (≥ activation energy) for a reaction
C) More particles means faster reactions always
D) Temperature only affects collision frequency
questions.explanation: Successful collisions require both correct orientation and sufficient energy — at least equal to the activation energy.

questions.question: What is a catalyst?
options: A) A reactant consumed in the reaction
B) A substance that speeds up a reaction by lowering activation energy — not consumed
C) A product of the reaction
D) A substance that slows reactions
questions.explanation: Catalysts provide an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy — more collisions are successful. They are not used up.

questions.question: What is an exothermic reaction?
options: A) A reaction that absorbs heat from surroundings
B) A reaction that releases heat — temperature of surroundings rises
C) A reaction that only releases light
D) A reaction requiring constant heat input
questions.explanation: Exothermic: energy released to surroundings — combustion, neutralisation, oxidation.

questions.question: What is fractional distillation?
options: A) Separating liquids by boiling point differences — crude oil heated, fractions condense at different temperatures
B) Cracking long hydrocarbons
C) Polymerisation of alkenes
D) Removing sulfur from oil
questions.explanation: Crude oil is heated — vapours rise up a fractionating column. Fractions with higher boiling points condense lower down; lighter fractions condense higher up.

questions.question: What are alkanes?
options: A) Hydrocarbons with double bonds
B) Saturated hydrocarbons with only single C-C bonds — CₙH₂ₙ₊₂
C) Products of cracking
D) Unsaturated hydrocarbons
questions.explanation: Alkanes are saturated — all C-C single bonds. Formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂: methane CH₄, ethane C₂H₆, propane C₃H₈.

questions.question: What is cracking?
options: A) Breaking crude oil into fractions by distillation
B) Breaking long-chain hydrocarbons into shorter, more useful molecules — produces alkenes
C) Polymerisation of hydrocarbons
D) Combustion of alkanes
questions.explanation: Cracking: thermal or catalytic decomposition of long alkane chains — produces shorter alkanes and alkenes (useful for fuels and polymers).

questions.question: How does temperature affect rate of reaction?
options: A) No effect
B) Higher temperature: more collisions with sufficient energy — faster rate
C) Higher temperature: fewer collisions — slower rate
D) Temperature only affects catalyst efficiency
questions.explanation: Higher temperature → particles move faster → more frequent collisions with higher energy → more successful collisions → faster rate.

questions.question: What is activation energy?
options: A) Energy released in a reaction
B) Energy stored in reactants
C) Minimum energy needed for a successful collision — lowered by a catalyst
D) Total energy of all particles
questions.explanation: Activation energy is the energy barrier a reaction must overcome. Catalysts lower this barrier — more particles have enough energy to react.

questions.question: What is crude oil?
options: A) A pure hydrocarbon
B) A mixture of hydrocarbons from ancient marine organisms — a fossil fuel
C) A product of cracking
D) Only used for making plastics
questions.explanation: Crude oil is a finite resource — formed over millions of years. It is a mixture of hydrocarbons that must be separated by fractional distillation.

questions.question: What is an endothermic reaction?
options: A) A reaction that releases heat
B) A reaction that absorbs heat from surroundings — temperature drops
C) Combustion of any fuel
D) Neutralisation reactions
questions.explanation: Endothermic: energy absorbed from surroundings — thermal decomposition, photosynthesis, dissolving ammonium nitrate.

questions.question: Particles must have sufficient energy for a successful collision.
questions.explanation: Activation energy is the minimum energy required for a successful collision.

questions.question: Catalysts are consumed during the reaction.
questions.explanation: Catalysts are NOT consumed — they speed up reactions by lowering activation energy.

questions.question: Exothermic reactions absorb heat from surroundings.
questions.explanation: Exothermic reactions RELEASE heat — temperature of surroundings rises.

questions.question: Alkanes have the general formula CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.
questions.explanation: Alkanes are saturated hydrocarbons with single C-C bonds only.

questions.question: Fractional distillation separates crude oil by density.
questions.explanation: Fractional distillation separates by BOILING POINT — different fractions condense at different temperatures.

questions.question: Cracking produces alkenes.
questions.explanation: Cracking breaks long alkane chains — producing shorter alkanes and unsaturated alkenes.

questions.question: Increasing temperature increases the activation energy.
questions.explanation: Increasing temperature does NOT change activation energy — it increases the number of particles with energy ≥ activation energy.

questions.question: Crude oil is a renewable resource.
questions.explanation: Crude oil is a FINITE, non-renewable fossil fuel.

questions.question: Endothermic reactions show a temperature decrease in the surroundings.
questions.explanation: Energy absorbed from surroundings → surroundings cool down.

questions.question: A catalyst provides an alternative reaction pathway with lower activation energy.
questions.explanation: This is why catalysts speed up reactions — more collisions are successful.

questions.question: ________ theory: particles must collide with ≥ activation energy for a reaction.
questions.explanation: Successful collisions require correct orientation AND sufficient energy.

questions.question: A ________ lowers activation energy — not consumed in the reaction.
questions.explanation: Catalysts provide an alternative lower-energy pathway.

questions.question: ________ reactions release heat — temperature of surroundings rises.
questions.explanation: Combustion, neutralisation, and oxidation are exothermic.

questions.question: ________ reactions absorb heat — temperature of surroundings falls.
questions.explanation: Thermal decomposition and dissolving ammonium nitrate are endothermic.

questions.question: ________ distillation separates crude oil fractions by boiling point.
questions.explanation: Fractions with high boiling points condense lower in the column.

questions.question: ________ are saturated hydrocarbons — only single C-C bonds — CₙH₂ₙ₊₂.
questions.explanation: Methane (CH₄), ethane (C₂H₆), propane (C₃H₈).

questions.question: ________ breaks long-chain hydrocarbons into shorter molecules and alkenes.
questions.explanation: Produces useful fuels and monomers for polymers.

questions.question: ________ energy is the minimum energy required for a successful collision.
questions.explanation: Catalysts lower activation energy.

questions.question: ________ oil is a finite mixture of hydrocarbons — separated by fractional distillation.
questions.explanation: Formed from ancient marine organisms over millions of years.

questions.question: Higher temperature means more particles have energy ≥ ________ energy.
questions.explanation: More successful collisions = faster rate of reaction.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: particles must have ≥ activation energy to react
separates crude oil by boiling point

cards.definition: the unit of amount of substance — 1 mole = 6.02 × 10²³ particles — moles = mass ÷ Mr
actual yield ÷ theoretical yield × 100% — measures efficiency of a reaction
Mr of desired product ÷ sum of Mr of all products × 100% — measures sustainability
amount of solute in a given volume — concentration = moles ÷ volume (mol/dm³)
the reactant that runs out first — determines the maximum amount of product
a reaction that can go forwards and backwards — shown by the ⇌ symbol
forward and reverse reactions occur at the same rate — concentrations remain constant
if a system at equilibrium is disturbed, it shifts to oppose the change
industrial synthesis of ammonia: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ — iron catalyst, 450°C, 200 atm
an acid that fully ionises in solution — e.g. HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃

questions.question: What is percentage yield?
options: A) The purity of a product
B) Actual yield ÷ theoretical yield × 100%
C) The atom economy of a reaction
D) The concentration of a product
questions.explanation: Percentage yield measures how much of the maximum possible product was obtained — always less than 100% in practice.

questions.question: What is atom economy?
options: A) Number of atoms in a reactant
B) Mr of desired product ÷ sum of Mr of all products × 100%
C) The moles of reactant used
D) Percentage yield of the reaction
questions.explanation: High atom economy = more atoms end up in the desired product = less waste. Desirable for sustainable chemistry.

questions.question: What is a limiting reactant?
options: A) The reactant present in the largest amount
B) The reactant that is always completely used — regardless of other reactants
C) The reactant that runs out first — determines maximum product
D) A catalyst
questions.explanation: The limiting reactant is used up completely — once it's gone, the reaction stops even if other reactants remain.

questions.question: What is dynamic equilibrium?
options: A) A reaction that has stopped
B) When forward and reverse reactions occur at the same rate — concentrations stay constant
C) When all reactants have been converted to products
D) A reaction with a very fast rate
questions.explanation: Dynamic equilibrium: the reaction has not stopped — forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates. Concentrations remain constant but not equal.

questions.question: What does Le Chatelier's principle state?
options: A) Catalysts shift equilibrium position
B) Increasing temperature always favours the forward reaction
C) If equilibrium is disturbed, the system shifts to oppose the change
D) Pressure only affects equilibrium of solids
questions.explanation: Le Chatelier's principle: increase concentration → equilibrium shifts to use it up. Increase temperature → shifts towards endothermic direction. Increase pressure → shifts towards fewer moles of gas.

questions.question: What are the conditions for the Haber process?
options: A) 200°C, 10 atm, platinum catalyst
B) 450°C, 200 atm, iron catalyst
C) 1000°C, 100 atm, iron catalyst
D) 50°C, 500 atm, nickel catalyst
questions.explanation: Haber process: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃. Conditions are a compromise — 450°C and 200 atm give good yield at acceptable rate.

questions.question: What is concentration in chemistry?
options: A) Mass of solute in grams
B) Amount of solute per unit volume — moles ÷ volume (mol/dm³)
C) Mass of solution in grams
D) Volume of solution in dm³
questions.explanation: Concentration = moles ÷ volume (dm³). Units: mol/dm³ or g/dm³.

questions.question: Why is percentage yield always less than 100%?
options: A) Some product is always destroyed
B) Reversible reactions, product loss in transfer, impure reactants — practical losses
C) Atom economy prevents 100% yield
D) Catalysts reduce yield
questions.explanation: In practice: reversible reactions don't go to completion, some product is lost in transfers, and reactants may not be 100% pure.

questions.question: What effect does increasing pressure have on a gas-phase equilibrium?
options: A) No effect
B) Shifts equilibrium towards the side with more moles of gas
C) Shifts equilibrium towards the side with fewer moles of gas
D) Always favours the reverse reaction
questions.explanation: Increasing pressure favours fewer moles of gas — the system shifts to reduce pressure.

questions.question: What is a strong acid?
options: A) An acid with a very low pH only
B) An acid that fully ionises in solution — e.g. HCl, H₂SO₄
C) A concentrated acid
D) An acid that reacts with all metals
questions.explanation: Strong acids fully ionise: HCl → H⁺ + Cl⁻ (100%). Weak acids partially ionise: CH₃COOH ⇌ CH₃COO⁻ + H⁺.

questions.question: Percentage yield measures efficiency of a reaction — always ≤ 100%.
questions.explanation: Practical losses (reversible reactions, transfers, impurities) mean yield is always below 100%.

questions.question: High atom economy means more waste is produced.
questions.explanation: HIGH atom economy means LESS waste — more atoms end up in the desired product.

questions.question: The limiting reactant is present in the largest amount.
questions.explanation: The limiting reactant runs out FIRST — it may not be the one in the largest amount by mass.

questions.question: At dynamic equilibrium, the reaction has stopped.
questions.explanation: At dynamic equilibrium, forward and reverse reactions continue at EQUAL RATES.

questions.question: Increasing temperature always increases yield.
questions.explanation: Increasing temperature shifts equilibrium towards the endothermic direction — may increase or decrease yield depending on the reaction.

questions.question: The Haber process uses an iron catalyst at 450°C.
questions.explanation: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃. Iron catalyst, 450°C, 200 atm.

questions.question: Concentration = moles ÷ volume.
questions.explanation: Units: mol/dm³. Volume must be in dm³.

questions.question: Le Chatelier's principle: equilibrium shifts to oppose disturbances.
questions.explanation: If temperature, pressure, or concentration changes, the equilibrium position shifts to partially counteract it.

questions.question: Increasing pressure favours the side with more moles of gas.
questions.explanation: Increasing pressure favours FEWER moles of gas — the system reduces pressure.

questions.question: Strong acids fully ionise in solution.
questions.explanation: HCl, H₂SO₄, and HNO₃ are strong acids — they fully dissociate in water.

questions.question: ________ = mass ÷ Mr — the unit of amount of substance.
questions.explanation: 1 mole contains 6.02 × 10²³ particles (Avogadro's constant).

questions.question: ________ yield = actual yield ÷ theoretical yield × 100%.
questions.explanation: Always ≤ 100% due to practical losses.

questions.question: ________ economy = Mr of desired product ÷ sum of Mr of all products × 100%.
questions.explanation: High atom economy = less waste = more sustainable.

questions.question: The ________ reactant runs out first — determines maximum product.
questions.explanation: Other reactants remain in excess.

questions.question: ________ equilibrium: forward and reverse reactions at equal rates — concentrations constant.
questions.explanation: The reaction has NOT stopped — both directions continue.

questions.question: Le ________'s principle: equilibrium shifts to oppose any disturbance.
questions.explanation: Increase pressure → shift to fewer moles of gas.

questions.question: The ________ process makes ammonia: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ — 450°C, 200 atm, iron.
questions.explanation: Ammonia is used to make fertilisers.

questions.question: A ________ acid fully ionises in solution — e.g. HCl, H₂SO₄.
questions.explanation: Strong ≠ concentrated. Strong refers to degree of ionisation.

questions.question: ________ = moles ÷ volume — measured in mol/dm³.
questions.explanation: Volume must be in dm³ (1 dm³ = 1 litre).

questions.question: Increasing pressure in a gas equilibrium shifts it towards ________ moles of gas.
questions.explanation: The system reduces pressure by shifting to the side with fewer gas molecules.

questions.instruction: Match each term to its correct description.

pairs.match: desired Mr ÷ all products Mr × 100% — sustainability
